rescue_reasoning: Keep as climate-relevant mitigation via transportation demand reduction enabled by land-use policy. Source text supports the intervention: "increase the types of home-based businesses allowed in residential districts." Parent statement provides explicit climate-adjacent rationale through urban form: "increasing density, diversity of land uses, and location efficiency," which aligns with reducing commute travel/VMT. Although no explicit mitigation outcome token (e.g., emissions reduction) appears, the provided mechanism (home_based_business_zoning_expansion → commute_vehicle_miles_reduction) is consistent with the policy intent, and climate_screen is "explicit_self" which overrides the primary gate failure.
policy_statement: By 2023, increase the types of home-based businesses allowed in residential districts (TLU.6-3).
mechanism_description: Increase the types of home-based businesses allowed in residential districts to enable more work-from-home/local work and reduce commuting travel and related emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Land-use regulation change → ↑ home-based/local work → ↓ commute trips/VMT → ↓ transport fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: The city relaxes/expands zoning and permitting rules so more business activities can legally operate from homes in residential areas. (2) Mediating process: More residents can work from home or access services/jobs locally, which substitutes for longer commute trips and reduces auto travel demand (VMT) and associated fuel use. (3) Outcome: Lower CO₂ (and some co-pollutant) emissions from reduced transportation energy consumption.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced commuting VMT that lowers on-road fuel combustion and tailpipe GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s main effect is transportation demand reduction (↓ commuting VMT) leading to ↓ fuel combustion and ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can incidentally improve resource efficiency (less energy for travel) or have economic co-benefits, the defining climate-relevant impact is direct emissions reduction from avoided travel—i.e., mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If home-based business expansion increases local delivery trips, induces additional non-commute travel, or increases residential energy use, these are secondary/variable effects and do not override the dominant intended pathway of commute reduction.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism specifically relies on enabling home-based/local work via zoning to reduce commuting travel; therefore mitigation remains the consistent primary category.  
5) Note on subtypes: Any secondary classification (e.g., transport demand management vs. land-use/urban form) is deferred to policy-level evidence and quantified impacts where available.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential rebound/offsets (more home energy use, increased delivery/errand trips, business-related visitor traffic) could reduce net savings in some contexts, but the canonical mechanism’s dominant effect remains commute VMT reduction and associated mitigation.
key_indicators: “increase the types of home-based businesses allowed”; “allowed in residential districts”; “By 2023”
co_benefits: Local economic development; small business formation/entrepreneurship; reduced traffic congestion and air pollutants; improved work flexibility
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be implemented as a zoning code amendment (a regulatory change), but the text is framed as a land-use planning allowance/permission change rather than an explicit mandate or standard.
